eli3: A norm measures the 'size' or 'length' of a vector. The most common is Euclidean (L2): √(x² + y² + z²) — straight-line distance. L1 norm |x| + |y| + |z| is Manhattan distance (grid walking).

Think of norms as different ways to measure distance. L2 prefers many small values; L1 prefers sparsity (some zeros). L∞ (max norm) only cares about the largest component. Each is useful in different contexts.
Norms define 'balls' — regions of points within a certain distance. L2 balls are round, L1 balls are diamond-shaped, L∞ balls are squares. These shapes affect optimization and regularization.

whenWhy.title: Regularization in machine learning
whenWhy.body: L2 regularization (Ridge) penalizes ||w||², encouraging small weights. L1 regularization (Lasso) penalizes ||w||₁, encouraging sparsity (feature selection). Elastic Net combines both.

whenWhy.title: Measuring prediction error
whenWhy.body: MSE uses L2 norm of errors: ||y - ŷ||². MAE uses L1 norm: ||y - ŷ||₁. L2 is differentiable everywhere; L1 is more robust to outliers. Different norms give different loss landscapes.

whenWhy.title: Convergence and stopping criteria
whenWhy.body: In iterative optimization (gradient descent, Newton's method), we check ||∇f|| < ε to detect convergence. The choice of norm affects when we stop. L∞ checks maximum component; L2 checks overall magnitude.

whenWhy.title: Matrix norms and condition numbers
whenWhy.body: ||A|| measures how much a matrix can stretch vectors. The condition number κ(A) = ||A|| ||A⁻¹|| measures sensitivity to numerical errors. Spectral norm (largest singular value) is common for matrices.

deepDiveIntro: A norm on a vector space V is a function ||·||: V → ℝ satisfying: (1) ||v|| ≥ 0 with equality iff v = 0; (2) ||αv|| = |α| ||v|| (homogeneity); (3) ||v + w|| ≤ ||v|| + ||w|| (triangle inequality). Norms induce metrics: d(v,w) = ||v - w||.

The Lp norms for p ≥ 1 are defined as ||x||ₚ = (Σᵢ |xᵢ|ᵖ)^(1/p). Special cases: L1 (Manhattan), L2 (Euclidean), L∞ = maxᵢ |xᵢ| (limit as p → ∞). For matrices, induced (operator) norms are defined as ||A|| = max_{x≠0} ||Ax||/||x||.

deepDive.heading: Lp Norms
deepDive.body: Different norms measure vector size in different ways. The two-norm emphasizes overall length, the one-norm adds absolute magnitudes and tends to encourage sparse solutions, and the infinity norm only cares about the largest component. Changing the norm changes the geometry of distance, optimization, and regularization.

deepDive.heading: Dual Norms
deepDive.body: Every norm has a companion dual norm that describes the largest inner product you can get using a vector of unit size under the original norm. This idea is central in optimization because it links linear objectives, constraints, and penalties. Familiar pairings include the one-norm with the infinity norm and the two-norm with itself.

deepDive.heading: Matrix Norms
deepDive.body: Matrix norms measure the size of a linear transformation rather than just the size of a list of numbers. The Frobenius norm summarizes all entries at once, while the spectral norm captures the largest stretching effect on any input vector. Different matrix norms matter depending on whether you care about total energy or worst-case amplification.

deepDive.heading: Unit Balls and Geometry
deepDive.body: Each norm creates its own unit ball, and the shape of that ball explains the behavior of optimization problems built on the norm. Round balls lead to smooth trade-offs, while balls with sharp corners make it easier for solutions to land exactly on coordinate axes. That geometric picture explains why one-norm penalties often produce sparse solutions.

deepDive.heading: Norm Equivalence
deepDive.body: In a finite-dimensional space, different norms may assign different numerical sizes but they agree on the big qualitative questions such as boundedness and convergence. A sequence that converges under one norm will also converge under any other. The practical difference is in the comparison constants, which can become large as dimension grows.

takeaways: Norms measure vector 'size' satisfying positivity, homogeneity, triangle inequality.
L2 (Euclidean): rotationally invariant, differentiable. L1: promotes sparsity.
Matrix Frobenius norm = √(tr(AᵀA)). Spectral norm = largest singular value.
Dual norms appear in optimization duality; L1 and L∞ are dual.
All norms equivalent in finite dimensions, but constants matter in practice.

edgeCases.q: Why does L1 promote sparsity but L2 doesn't?
edgeCases.a: The L1 unit ball has sharp corners on the axes. When minimizing L1 subject to constraints, the solution tends to hit these corners (sparse). L2's round ball doesn't have corners — solutions are typically dense. Also, L1's gradient is constant magnitude, so small coefficients get pushed to zero.

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between Frobenius and spectral norms?
edgeCases.a: Frobenius treats the matrix as a vector and takes L2 norm of all entries. Spectral norm measures maximum stretching factor: max ||Ax||/||x||. Frobenius ≥ spectral always. Frobenius is easier to differentiate; spectral captures worst-case behavior.

L0 counts nonzero entries. It's not a true norm (violates homogeneity: ||2x||₀ = ||x||₀). Minimizing L0 is NP-hard (combinatorial). L1 is the convex relaxation of L0, making sparse recovery tractable via convex optimization.
